The Many Small But Critical Details of Inviting Speakers
This post mostly records the small details when I invite speakers to talk at events. We tend to overlook these details.
Since the topic is decided by the boss, I don't have to think much about this.
Usually we find speakers based on the topic. Before searching, I brainstorm who I already know that might fit.
If the topic is harder or too narrow, it's hard to think of someone from memory.
How do I find speakers? Common channels:
Is finding the speaker enough? No. We do online livestreams, so I prioritize speakers who've previously shared on topics related to ours. So how do you pick suitable speakers?
After landing on candidates A, B, C, the first considerations are:
Have they shared a video or done a livestream before?
Have they given in-person talks before?
Have they shared a video or done a livestream?

Some speakers are turned off by livestreaming. Livestreaming has no geographic limits, but some speakers prefer in-person talks. So if a speaker has shared video / livestream before, they're less averse to livestream — higher invite success rate.

If the candidate hasn't done video / livestream, check whether they've spoken in person. They need to face a large audience, so if they haven't shared video AND haven't given in-person talks, the invite is likely to fail.
If there's no contact method, they get filtered out right away.
Once you have A, B, C candidates that meet the criteria, time to invite. The invite step is even more important than the steps above.
I'd already filtered out anyone with no contact method, so we have ways to reach them. There are details in how to reach out. Common channels:
When inviting via Facebook, understand what the platform is for. Facebook is a social platform, so people use it as a relaxation space — sending an invite directly is somewhat off.
For example, we mostly check Messenger. When you send invite info there, the speaker's Messenger looks like this:
You can see Facebook invites get bulky fast — so it's not great.
But if a speaker's only contact channel is Facebook, what then? Just send the main reason for the invite — at most three paragraphs. Two main details to include:

Point 1 is simpler — you found them because of the topic. Point 2 takes more time. Say you found them on YouTube — you need to mention which part of which video impressed you and made you want to invite them.
I personally watch every video the speaker has shared and read every post. Then I message them based on something they shared, saying
was excellent, so I'd like to invite you"
— and pinpoint which segment of the video drove the invite. This shows respect for the speaker, because what they share is valuable knowledge (honestly, I've learned a lot from this process).
Eventually you need their email, so Facebook contact is mainly about explaining the invite reason — the goal is getting their email.

Email is more formal, both for record-keeping and as a more proper invite channel — respectful to the speaker. There are details in writing the email. The classic "open / develop / turn / wrap" structure works well. Example.
Start with a greeting, then introduce your organization:

Hi A,
I'm Ray from HexSchool in Kaohsiung.
Then say what you want to invite them to:
We'd like to invite you to participate in our event, the topic is xxx. Here's our speaker invitation letter ~
Then how you found them, and why:

While searching for speakers, we found your Udemy course XXX, which introduces many practical concepts. The one that really stood out for me was the part at X min X sec of [video name] — it suddenly resolved a question I'd had for years. We feel you'd be a great fit for our topic!
I'm not exaggerating — sometimes searching for speakers actually solves my own long-standing questions.
Then tell the speaker about your audience:
Our main audience is xx, so we hope your share will help our audience understand these concepts and see what's worth exploring further.
Then tell them the timing and the tool you'll livestream with:
The session length is 90 minutes. We livestream via Zoom — before the stream, a host will bring you into the room and walk you through controls, so no worries at all ~
Combine into a complete invite email:

Hi A,
I'm Ray from HexSchool in Kaohsiung.
We'd like to invite you to participate in our event, the topic is xxx. Here's our speaker invitation letter ~

While searching for speakers, we found your Udemy course XXX, which introduces many practical concepts. The one that really stood out for me was the part at X min X sec of [video name] — it suddenly resolved a question I'd had for years. We feel you'd be a great fit for our topic!

Our main audience is xx, so we hope your share will help our audience understand these concepts and see what's worth exploring further.

The session length is 90 minutes. We livestream via Zoom — before the stream, a host will bring you into the room and walk you through controls, so no worries at all ~
A speaker invitation letter usually covers:
The letter should tell the speaker the purpose of holding the event, e.g., "make the world a better place" (?????)
Every talk has a topic — this represents the event's main focus.
The outline is provided by our side. If the speaker feels the outline isn't great to work with, they can revise it — as long as the topic doesn't drift.
Timing needs to be confirmed. Every speaker has different availability, so we usually recommend giving the speaker a month to prepare materials — gives them adequate time to prepare polished content.
A few more reminders. Once the speaker is on board, confirm:
The speaker bio matters because we promote the speaker externally. If you invite a speaker and don't promote them, would they really come?
I skipped some details that are tied to internal company stuff. Final note — I'll include a few really solid articles about inviting speakers.
